design_philosophy.core_loop: Place falling tetrominoes to complete horizontal lines; cleared lines disappear and grant points based on efficiency (single/double/triple/Tetris). Speed increases with level, creating escalating pressure.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Randomized piece order vs. planned placement; speed acceleration forces players toward high-efficiency clears (Tetrises) within narrowing time windows; designed endpoint at level 29 'kill screen' creates predetermined finish line.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Achieve mastery through pattern recognition and swift decision-making; accumulate high score through skillful play before game becomes unplayably fast; progress from casual play to competitive optimization.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Intentionally designed with a hard cap—level 29 'kill screen' intended to be unbeatable, making score accumulation the true victory condition rather than endless survival. Rewards efficiency over endurance. Scoring multiplier system heavily favors Tetris (4-line clear) over singles, pushing players toward higher-risk play. Two distinct modes (A-Type vs B-Type) serve different player philosophies: score-chasing vs obstacle-clearing challenges.

difficulty.continues_system: Single continuous game session; no continues or lives; game ends when player tops out (fills screen) or reaches kill screen at level 29
difficulty.checkpointing: No checkpoints; full-game progression from level 0/selected starting level to completion or topout. Starting level selection (0-9, or 0-19 with A-button hold) provides player-selected difficulty entry point.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: players choose starting level (0-19 range); A-Type vs B-Type mode selection; starting level effectively gates difficulty progression. No difficulty modifiers once game begins. Speed curves differ between North American and European/PAL versions due to frame rate conversion.

progression.unlock_mechanism: Level advancement: A-Type increases level every 10 lines cleared (levels 0-29); speed increases at levels 1-10, then 13, 16, 19, 29. B-Type has fixed level but players select starting obstacle height. Speed curve creates natural progression gates: level 29 intended as hard stop.
replayability_hooks: High score chasing with persistent leaderboard (tracks top scores with player names)
Two distinct game modes (A-Type score-driven vs B-Type obstacle-clearing) require different strategies
Starting level selection creates multiple entry points and difficulty curves
Randomized piece order ensures no two games play identically
Competitive speedrun/optimization potential: novel button-mashing techniques discovered in 2020s extended gameplay beyond intended limits
A-Type endings scale with score (bottle rocket to UFO), rewarding efficiency
B-Type post-game Nintendo character parade adds cosmetic reward to high scores

world_building_techniques: Saint Basil's Cathedral appears in title screen and ending sequences, alluding to Soviet origin of Tetris intellectual property
Rocket launch sequence (bottle rocket → Buran spaceplane → UFO) scales with player score, creating visual narrative of achievement
Nintendo character cameos in B-Type high-score ending serve as celebratory fan-service rather than narrative
Title screen silent (unused music track was presumably intended for title), keeping focus on gameplay

danger_signals: Level 29 'kill screen': pieces fall at 1 grid cell per frame—intended as unbeatable endpoint; modern players have overcome this with novel techniques, signaling mastery threshold
environmental_storytelling: Saint Basil's Cathedral backdrop connects gameplay to Soviet cultural context (Tetris creator Alexey Pajitnov origin)
Rocket progression (bottle rocket → space shuttle → UFO) visually communicates player achievement tier
Score multiplier system (Tetris worth 1200 points vs single worth 40) teaches players that 'perfect play' is rewarded over mere survival

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - gameplay mechanics, kill screen explanation, speed techniques, competitive history
wikipedia - scoring system, high-level play techniques, kill screen technical explanation, esports legacy
